title: Lab Five-Six – Data Wrangling in R
Lab Five-Six – Data Wrangling in R
text:
1.1 Data Wrangling

1.1.1 Part A
Go to the website and download the source data https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-68063-y#Sec30.
Describe the process – do not leave out any steps – for how you can load the data for Figures 3b and 3d from the manuscript into R.
Hint: You can use packages like readxl (Wickham and Bryan 2025) but you will find it substantially easier to open the .xls file using your computer’s spreadsheet software and create a new .csv file with the data you need.
Solution: To accomplish this task, I opened the link provided and downloaded the source data. I then opened the data using Microsoft Excel and navigated to the tab entitled Fig 3b. I then appended the duplicates of each column so that there is only one column entitled “sample” and one column entitled “viruses within nucleus” I then clicked file, save as, renamed it to "Lab5Fig3B", selected .csv as the format, and saved it to the my folder for Labs 5 and 6.

After doing that, I load it in using the tidyverse (Wickham et al. 2019) function, read_csv() and the full file path, skipping the blank rows.



Code
dat.kitsberg = read_csv(
  "/Users/alexvogt/Documents/GitHub/lab-5-6-data-wrangling-alvogt1020/Lab5Fig3B.csv",
  skip = 1
)





1.1.2 Part B
The data need to be cleaned. Note that the sample type (Healthy Donor or THP-1), cell type (Monocytes or Macrophages), and treatment (12 Hours Post-Infection or Uninfected) are all recorded in one column sample, separated with underscores. Use separate(...) from the tidyverse package to separate the sample column into the SampleType, CellType, Treatment columns. Save the object as dat.kitsberg.cleaned.
Hint: Use ?separate(...) to see how it works.
Solution: I fill in the separate(...) function with the appropriate arguments to break up sample into multiple based on the criteria given.



Code
dat.kitsberg.cleaned = separate(
  data = dat.kitsberg,
  col = "sample",
  into = c("SampleType","CellType","Treatment"),
  sep = "_"
)

dat.kitsberg.cleaned = dat.kitsberg.cleaned[, 1:4] # eliminate NA columns





1.1.3 Part C
The researchers are only interested in observations in the treatment group (12hpi). Remove uninfected samples from the data. Update dat.kitsberg.cleaned with the result.
Hint: I would use the filter(...) function.
Solution: I use the filter(...) function to select only observations for which Treatment takes on the value “12hpi”.



Code
dat.kitsberg.cleaned = dplyr::filter(
  dat.kitsberg.cleaned,
  Treatment == "12hpi"
)





1.1.4 Part D
When CellType is “MDM”, it is a Monocyte-Derived Macrophages. That is, the cell type should be macrophages which is coded as “mac”. Update dat.kitsberg.cleaned with the result.
Solution: I feed dat.kitsberg.cleaned into the mutate(...) function, which uses a conditional statement to recode MDM in CellType to mac.



Code
dat.kitsberg.cleaned = dat.kitsberg.cleaned |>
  mutate(
    CellType = if_else(CellType == "MDM","mac",CellType)
  )



Hint: I would use the mutate(...) function. You will likely find if_else(...) or case_when(...) helpful.

title: Lab Five-Six – Data Wrangling in R
text:
1.2 Part E
The researchers are only interested in monocytes and macrophages. Remove any other cell types. Update dat.kitsberg.cleaned with the result.
Hint: I would use the filter(...) function.
Solution: I use View(...) to find the appropriate observation names, and the filter function from dplyr to rename the statements based on a logical or condition.



Code
dat.kitsberg.cleaned = dplyr::filter(
                                 dat.kitsberg.cleaned, 
                                 CellType == "mac" | 
                                 CellType == "mono"
                                 )

title: Lab Five-Six – Data Wrangling in R
text:
1.3 Part F
Clean up the data entry. Replace the shorthand the researchers used with the neat sample type (Healthy Donor or THP-1), cell type (Monocytes or Macrophages), and treatment (12 Hours Post-Infection or Uninfected). Update dat.kitsberg.cleaned with the result.
Hint: I would use the mutate(...) function. You will likely find if_else(...) or case_when(...) helpful.
Solution:
I use View(...) to see what each observation is named so that I can use a series of if_else statements to rename the observations.



Code
dat.kitsberg.cleaned = dat.kitsberg.cleaned |> 
                      mutate(
                      SampleType = if_else(SampleType == "primary", "Healthy Donor",
                                   if_else(SampleType == "THP1","THP-1",SampleType)),
                      CellType   = if_else(CellType == "mono", "Monocytes",
                                   if_else(CellType == "mac", "Macrophages", CellType)),
                      Treatment  = if_else(Treatment == "12hpi", "12 Hours Post-Infection",
                                   if_else(Treatment == "uninfected", "Uninfected",Treatment))
                     )

title: Lab Five-Six – Data Wrangling in R
text:
1.4 Part G
In an R code block, combine your answers into one code block that completes the full cleaning process. Save the object as dat.kitsberg.cleaned.
Note: You will use this in the next section
Solution:



Code
# Loading in the data (part A)
dat.kitsberg = read_csv(
  "/Users/alexvogt/Documents/GitHub/lab-5-6-data-wrangling-alvogt1020/Lab5Fig3B.csv",
  skip = 1
)

# Separating columns (part B)
dat.kitsberg.cleaned = separate(
  data = dat.kitsberg,
  col = "sample",
  into = c("SampleType","CellType","Treatment"),
  sep = "_"
)

dat.kitsberg.cleaned = dat.kitsberg.cleaned[1:4] # eliminate NA columns

# Retaining only observations from the treatment group (part C)
dat.kitsberg.cleaned = dplyr::filter(
  dat.kitsberg.cleaned,
  Treatment == "12hpi"
)

# Coding "MDM" --> "mac" (Part D)
dat.kitsberg.cleaned = dat.kitsberg.cleaned |>
  mutate(
    CellType = if_else(CellType == "MDM","mac",CellType)
  )

# Retaining only monocyte and macrophage data (part E)
dat.kitsberg.cleaned = dplyr::filter(
                                 dat.kitsberg.cleaned, 
                                 CellType == "mac" | 
                                 CellType == "mono"
                                 )

# Replacing shorthand with neater format (Part F)
dat.kitsberg.cleaned = dat.kitsberg.cleaned |> 
                      mutate(
                      SampleType = if_else(SampleType == "primary", "Healthy Donor",
                                   if_else(SampleType == "THP1","THP-1",SampleType)),
                      CellType   = if_else(CellType == "mono", "Monocytes",
                                   if_else(CellType == "mac", "Macrophages", CellType)),
                      Treatment  = if_else(Treatment == "12hpi", "12 Hours Post-Infection",
                                   if_else(Treatment == "uninfected", "Uninfected",Treatment))
                     )

title: Lab Five-Six – Data Wrangling in R
text:
1.5 Data Summaries

1.5.1 Part A
Recreate Figure 3b as depicted in the paper (see https://www.nature.com/articles/s41467-025-68063-y#Fig3). You can ignore the significance comparison because we haven’t done that yet.
Hint: I would use geom_violin(...) for Figure 3b I also expect your plots to look nicer – better labels, better scaling, etc. Don’t forget to use the proper formatting using the syntax in the Quarto cheatsheet.
Solution:



Code
ggplot(data = dat.kitsberg.cleaned |> drop_na(SampleType)) + # data to plot
    geom_violin(aes(x = CellType, 
                    y = `viruses within nucleus`),
                fill = "lightgrey", alpha=0.5) +
    geom_boxplot(aes(x = CellType, 
                     y = `viruses within nucleus`), width = 0.1)+
    theme_bw() +
    labs(x = "Cell Type",
         y = "HCMV Genomes in the Nucleus")+
    facet_wrap(~SampleType)












1.5.2 Part B
Compute a table containing relevant numerical summaries for Figure 3b.
Hint: I would use group_by(...) and summarize(...) to complete this. Don’t forget to use the proper formatting using knitr::kable(...) as described in the Quarto Cheatsheet.
Solution:



Code
kitsberg.summary = dat.kitsberg.cleaned |>
  group_by(SampleType, CellType) |>
  summarize(
    n = n(),
    Missing = sum(is.na(`viruses within nucleus`)),
    Mean = mean(`viruses within nucleus`, na.rm = T),
    SD = sd(`viruses within nucleus`, na.rm = T),
    Median = median(`viruses within nucleus`, na.rm = T),
    IQR = IQR(`viruses within nucleus`, na.rm = T)
  )



To print the table nicely, we use the knitr::kable(...), as suggested.

From the numerical summary, we see that the average amount of HCMV genomes in the nucleus is significantly higher for macrophages than monocytes. Furthermore, macrophages exhibit much more spread with a standard deviation 3x that of monocytes. The violin plots further enrich our understanding of the results. We see that healthy donors in general exhibit more spread than THP-1 donors (which aligns with the intuitive explanation that humans are more varied than artificial cells).
Note: Parts C and D removed, to align with the revised version of the lab assignment.

title: Lab Five-Six – Data Wrangling in R
text:
2.1 Data Wrangling

2.1.1 Part A
I downloaded the data from linked in the article (https://journals.plos.org/plosone/article?id=10.1371/journal.pone.0319073). I have included the data in this lab folder as ShipValuation-Bal25.csv. Load the data into R.
Solution:



Code
dat.bal = read_csv("ShipValuation-Bal25.csv")





2.1.2 Part B
The price of each Supramax and Ultramax ship is reported on the \(\log_{10}\) scale. Create a new column called price that contains the price in millions of dollars. \[\text{Price} = \frac{10^{\text{SPLog}}}{1000000}\] Save the object as dat.bal.cleaned.
Hint: I would use the mutate(...) function.
Solution:



Code
dat.bal.cleaned = dat.bal |>
        mutate(
          price = 10 ^ SPLog / 1000000
        )





2.1.3 Part C
The authors recorded the country that each Supramax or Ultramax ship was built in in the columns Japan, China, People's Republic Of, Philippines, Korea, South, Others Country Build. These columns are marked 1 to indicate that is the country of origin and 0 otherwise. Create a new column called OriginCountry that contains a clean labels for the country in which the ship was built. Update dat.bal.cleaned with the result.
Hint: I would use the mutate(...) function. You will likely find if_else(...) or case_when(...) helpful.
Solution: Using case_when(...) in this instance is much easier given the amount of columns needed to scan as well as the character lengths.



Code
dat.bal.cleaned = dat.bal.cleaned |>
  mutate(
    OriginCountry = case_when(
      Japan == 1 ~ "Japan",
      `China, People's Republic Of` == 1 ~ "China",
      Philippines == 1 ~ "Philippines",
      `Korea, South` == 1 ~ "South Korea",
      `Others Country Build` == 1 ~ "Other",
      TRUE ~ NA
    )
  )





2.1.4 Part D
The authors recorded each Supramax and Ultramax ship’s main engine manufacturer in the columns Caterpillar, MAN-B&W, Wartsila, Mitsubishi. These columns are marked 1 to indicate that is the manufacturer and 0 otherwise. Create a new column called Manufacturer that contains a clean labels for the manufacturer. Update dat.bal.cleaned with the result.
Hint: Follow the logic of the previous question.
Solution:
Copy and pasting the code from part C and adjusting it is the quickest approach for this.



Code
dat.bal.cleaned = dat.bal.cleaned |>
  mutate(
    Manufacturer = case_when(
      Caterpillar == 1 ~ "Caterpillar",
      `MAN-B&W` == 1 ~ "MAN-B&W",
      Wartsila == 1 ~ "Wartsila",
      Mitsubishi == 1 ~ "Mitsubishi",
      TRUE ~ NA
    )
  )





2.1.5 Part E
The researchers recorded whether each Supramax and Ultramax ship is currently covered by a Protection and Indemnity (P&I) Club. This can be an indicator that a ship meets the minimum seaworthiness and safety requirements of the insurer.
The authors recorded P&I clubs in the columns Japan P&I Club, Gard, West of England, North of England, UK P&I, Britannia P&I, London P&I Club, Skuld, Steamship Mutual, Others, Unknown. These columns are marked 1 to indicate that the ship is insured by that P&I club and 0 otherwise. Create a new column called PI.club that contains a clean labels for the P&I club. Update dat.bal.cleaned with the result.
Hint: Follow the logic of the previous questions.
Solution:
Again, copy and pasting the code from part C and adjusting it is the quickest approach for this.



Code
dat.bal.cleaned = dat.bal.cleaned |>
  mutate(
    PI.Club = case_when(
      `Japan P&I Club` == 1 ~ "Japan P&I Club",
      `Gard` == 1 ~ "Gard",
      `West of England` == 1 ~ "West of England",
      `North of England` == 1 ~ "North of England",
      `UK P&I` == 1 ~ "UK P&I",
      `Britannia P&I` == 1 ~ "Britannia P&I",
      `London P&I Club`== 1 ~ "London P&I Club",
      `Skuld` == 1 ~ "Skuld",
      `Steamship Mutual` == 1 ~ "Steamship Mutual",
      `Others` == 1 ~ "Others",
      `Unknown` == 1 ~ "Unknown",
      TRUE ~ NA
    )
  )





2.1.6 Part F
The researchers recorded whether each Supramax and Ultramax ship is classed by a society that requires a ship to be safe, structurally sound, and built/maintained to specific engineering standards.
The authors recorded class society in the columns Nippon Kaiji Kyokai (IACS), Lloyd's Register, Bureau Veritas (IACS), Det Norske Veritas (IACS), American Bureau of Shipping (IACS), Others Class, With Two Classes, Disclassed. These columns are marked 1 to indicate that the ship is classed by that soceity and 0 otherwise. Create a new column called Class that contains a clean labels for the class. Update dat.bal.cleaned with the result.
Hint: Follow the logic of the previous questions.
Solution:
Again, copy and pasting the code from part C and adjusting it is the quickest approach for this.



Code
dat.bal.cleaned = dat.bal.cleaned |>
  mutate(
    Class = case_when(
      `Nippon Kaiji Kyokai (IACS)` == 1 ~ "Nippon Kaiji Kyokai (IACS)",
      `Lloyd's Register` == 1 ~ "Lloyd's Register",
      `Bureau Veritas (IACS)` == 1 ~ "Bureau Veritas (IACS)",
      `Det Norske Veritas (IACS)` == 1 ~ "Det Norske Veritas (IACS)",
      `American Bureau of Shipping (IACS)` == 1 ~ "American Bureau of Shipping (IACS)",
      `Others Class` == 1 ~ "Others Class",
      `With Two Classes`== 1 ~ "With Two Classes",
      `Disclassed` == 1 ~ "Disclassed",
      TRUE ~ NA
    )
  )





2.1.7 Part G
The researchers recorded the flag state of each Supramax and Ultramax ship. Each flag state enforces its own set of safety, environmental, and crew welfare rules and regulations. Ships under certain flag states may be more or less valuable depending on how much the flag affects the buyer’s trust in the condition of the boat.
The authors recorded flag states in the columns Panama, Marshall Islands, Liberia, Malta, Hong Kong, China, Singapore, Others Flag, With two flags. These columns are marked 1 to indicate that the ship has that flag state and 0 otherwise. Create a new column called FlagState that contains a clean labels for the flag state. Update dat.bal.cleaned with the result.
Hint: Follow the logic of the previous questions.
Solution:
Again, copy and pasting the code from part C and adjusting it is the quickest approach for this.



Code
dat.bal.cleaned = dat.bal.cleaned |>
  mutate(
    FlagState = case_when(
      `Panama` == 1 ~ "Panama",
      `Marshall Islands` == 1 ~ "Marshall Islands",
      `Liberia` == 1 ~ "Liberia",
      `Malta` == 1 ~ "Malta",
      `Hong Kong, China` == 1 ~ "Hong Kong",
      `Singapore` == 1 ~ "Singapore",
      `Others Flag` == 1 ~ "Others Flag",
      `With two flags`== 1 ~ "With two flags",
      TRUE ~ NA
    )
  )

title: Lab Five-Six – Data Wrangling in R
text:
2.2 Part H
In an R code block, combine your answers into one code block that completes the full cleaning process. Save the object as dat.bal.cleaned.
Note: You will use this in the next section



Code
# Loading the data (part A)
dat.bal = read_csv("ShipValuation-Bal25.csv")

# Creating price col (part B)
dat.bal.cleaned = dat.bal |>
        mutate(
          price = 10 ^ SPLog / 1000000
        )

# Creating OriginCountry (part C)
dat.bal.cleaned = dat.bal.cleaned |>
  mutate(
    OriginCountry = case_when(
      Japan == 1 ~ "Japan",
      `China, People's Republic Of` == 1 ~ "China",
      Philippines == 1 ~ "Philippines",
      `Korea, South` == 1 ~ "South Korea",
      `Others Country Build` == 1 ~ "Other",
      TRUE ~ NA
    )
  )

# Creating Manufacturer (part D)
dat.bal.cleaned = dat.bal.cleaned |>
  mutate(
    Manufacturer = case_when(
      Caterpillar == 1 ~ "Caterpillar",
      `MAN-B&W` == 1 ~ "MAN-B&W",
      Wartsila == 1 ~ "Wartsila",
      Mitsubishi == 1 ~ "Mitsubishi",
      TRUE ~ NA
    )
  )

# Creating PI.club (part E)
dat.bal.cleaned = dat.bal.cleaned |>
  mutate(
    PI.Club = case_when(
      `Japan P&I Club` == 1 ~ "Japan P&I Club",
      `Gard` == 1 ~ "Gard",
      `West of England` == 1 ~ "West of England",
      `North of England` == 1 ~ "North of England",
      `UK P&I` == 1 ~ "UK P&I",
      `Britannia P&I` == 1 ~ "Britannia P&I",
      `London P&I Club`== 1 ~ "London P&I Club",
      `Skuld` == 1 ~ "Skuld",
      `Steamship Mutual` == 1 ~ "Steamship Mutual",
      `Others` == 1 ~ "Others",
      `Unknown` == 1 ~ "Unknown",
      TRUE ~ NA
    )
  )

# Creating Class (part F)
dat.bal.cleaned = dat.bal.cleaned |>
  mutate(
    Class = case_when(
      `Nippon Kaiji Kyokai (IACS)` == 1 ~ "Nippon Kaiji Kyokai (IACS)",
      `Lloyd's Register` == 1 ~ "Lloyd's Register",
      `Bureau Veritas (IACS)` == 1 ~ "Bureau Veritas (IACS)",
      `Det Norske Veritas (IACS)` == 1 ~ "Det Norske Veritas (IACS)",
      `American Bureau of Shipping (IACS)` == 1 ~ "American Bureau of Shipping (IACS)",
      `Others Class` == 1 ~ "Others Class",
      `With Two Classes`== 1 ~ "With Two Classes",
      `Disclassed` == 1 ~ "Disclassed",
      TRUE ~ NA
    )
  )

# Creating FlagState (part G)
dat.bal.cleaned = dat.bal.cleaned |>
  mutate(
    FlagState = case_when(
      `Panama` == 1 ~ "Panama",
      `Marshall Islands` == 1 ~ "Marshall Islands",
      `Liberia` == 1 ~ "Liberia",
      `Malta` == 1 ~ "Malta",
      `Hong Kong, China` == 1 ~ "Hong Kong",
      `Singapore` == 1 ~ "Singapore",
      `Others Flag` == 1 ~ "Others Flag",
      `With two flags`== 1 ~ "With two flags",
      TRUE ~ NA
    )
  )

title: Lab Five-Six – Data Wrangling in R
text:
2.3 Data Summaries
First, I provide a short description of the other variables in their data. Note that the size ordering of types of ships discussed below is:
\[\text{Handysize} < \text{Supramax} < \text{Ultramax} < \text{Panamax} < \text{Capesize}\]

DWT: The deadweight tonnage, which is the maximum weight each Supramax and Ultramax ship can safely carry in metric tons. This gives a measure of how much cargo, fuel, fresh water, ballast water, provisions, and crew a ship can handle.
Yas: The age in years. New Supramax and Ultramax ships were recorded as having age 1.
Enginges RPM: Revolutions per minute of each Supramax and Ultramax ship’s main propulsion engine. Generally speaking, lower-RPM engines are more expensive because they are more fuel efficient.
BACI: The Baltic Capesize Index. This describes freight rates for Capesize dry bulk ships, which reflects supply and demand. When BACI is high, Capesize ships are more profitable in shipping, which could positively affect Supramax and Ultramax ship values as demand for dry bulk commodities might be high and large shipments could be split into several Supramax and Ultramax shipments.
Note: This reports the current BACI. The data also contain the BACI form 1, 2, and 3 months before the sale (BACI-1, BACI-2, BACI-3).
BPNI: The Baltic Panamax Index. This describes the cost to ship dry bulk commodities (e.g., coal or grain) on Panamax class ships. When BPNI is high, we expect Panamax ship prices to go up. Supramax and Ultramax ship values tend to follow this trend as charterers switch to these smaller vessels when Panamax rates become too expensive.
Note: This reports the current BPNI. The data also contain the BPNI form 1, 2, and 3 months before the sale (BPNI-1, BPNI-2, BPNI-3).
BSIS: The Baltic Supramax Index. This describes freight rates for Supramax vessels, which reflects supply and demand. When BSIS is high, Supramax and Ultramax prices are usually higher because they are more profitable in shipping.
Note: This reports the current BSIS. The data also contain the BSIS form 1, 2, and 3 months before the sale (BSIS-1, BSIS-2, BSIS-3).
BHSI: The Baltic Handysize Index. This describes freight rates for handysize vessels. When BHSI is high, Supramax and Ultramax ship prices are usually higher because the prices move together.
Note: This reports the current BHSI. The data also contain the BHSI form 1, 2, and 3 months before the sale (BHSI-1, BHSI-2, BHSI-3).
LIBOR: The London InterBank Offered Rate. This describes the average reported interest rate at which banks would lend US dollars for 3 months.


2.3.1 Part A
Choose a categorical variable. Create a plot and corresponding numerical summary to assess whether or how ship prices vary across the levels of the selected categorical variable.
Solution: For the visualization of any categorical variable, I choose to analyze the variable OriginCountry. I use a box plot to compare price across categories.



Code
ggplot(dat.bal.cleaned) +                                    # Specify Data
  geom_boxplot(aes(x = OriginCountry,                            # Specify x-axis
               y = price)) +                                 # Specify y-axis
  theme_bw() +                                               # A cleaner theme
  labs(x = "Country of Origin",                              # Specify axis labels
       y = "Price (Millions of $)") +
  ggtitle("Ship Price by Country of Origin in Ship Valuation Data") # Plot title










I then produce a corresponding numerical summary:



Code
bal.summary = dat.bal.cleaned |>
  group_by(OriginCountry) |>
  summarize(
    n = n(),
    `Missing Price` = sum(is.na(price)),
    `Mean Price` = mean(price, na.rm = TRUE),
    `SD Price` = sd(price, na.rm = TRUE),
    `Median Price` = median(price, na.rm = TRUE),
    `IQR Price` = IQR(price, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

knitr::kable(bal.summary)

From the numerical summary, we see that the Phillipines has the highest mean price (around 17 million dollars), followed by Japan and China (16.86 and 16.8 million dollars respectively). Countries that fall under other have a noticeably lower average price of around 11.8 million dollars. The median prices are more similar than the means, though “other” countries and South Korea have very low medians (9.4 and 11.2 million dollars respectively).
We can also see that Japan has the highest variability in terms of standard deviation and IQR, with “other” countries showing the least variability.


2.3.2 Part B
Choose a quantitative variable. Create a plot and corresponding numerical summary to assess whether or how ship prices vary with the selected quantitative variable.
Solution: For the visualization of any quantitative/continuous variable, I choose to analyze the variable LIBOR, which reports the average interest rate at which banks would lend US dollars for 3 months. I use a scatterplot to visualize the relationship between this and price.



Code
ggplot(dat.bal.cleaned) +                     
  geom_point(aes(x = `LIBOR`,                 
                 y =  price), alpha = 0.3) +   
  geom_smooth(aes(x = `LIBOR`,                
                  y = price),                 
              method = "lm",                  
              color = "black") +              
  theme_bw() +                                
  labs(x = "London InterBank Offered Rate (LIBOR)",         
       y = "Price (Millions of $)") +
  ggtitle("Price against LIBOR")                            










Note that an alpha of 0.3 is specified to add transparency to our points. This helps tackle the problem of densely concentrated data (particulary close to 0.00).
To create the numerical summary, we use the code below:





Table 2: Ship Price vs LIBOR in Ship Valuation Data

We can see that there is a weak, positive, linear correlation between LIBOR and ship price (corr = 0.323471). The scatterplot shows that most observations occur with LIBOR values close to 0 (mean LIBOR = .0149213). The average price of ships from this dataset is around 16.5 million dollars, with a significant amount of spread (SD = 11.04333).
